data.title: FPX: High-Beta Growth Tech Will Persist
data.description:
The First Trust US Equity Opportunities ETF (NYSEARCA:FPX) is an exchange-traded fund designed to provide investors with exposure to the 100 largest, most liquid, and best-performing US companies that have recently become listed on the public markets through initial public offerings [IPOs]. FPX charges a moderate expense ratio of 61bps and has $1.16b in net assets. FPX can be considered a high-beta growth portfolio and may be used as a risk-on investment strategy. Though FPX pays out a modest distribution rate of $0.74/share, I would not consider this strategy for its income component given that it yields a mere 0.44%.

About First Trust US Equity Opportunities ETF

FPX was launched by First Trust Advisors on April 12, 2006 on the NYSE Arca Exchange. FPX can be considered as a high-growth strategy with a beta of 1.35x and a portfolio price/earnings [P/E] ratio of 43.38x, presenting investors with a portfolio of risk-on investments.

The strategy is indexed to the IPOX US Composite Index, a market cap-weighted index that tracks the performance of newly publicly listed companies. The Index may include companies that became public entities through IPOs and spin-offs within the last 4 years. The Index caps constituent weight at 10% to improve diversification, given that companies may range in size from small- to large-cap. The Index is rebalanced quarterly. The strategy largely favors large-cap companies but balances out diversification through the cap on constituent weights. Company sizes range from $772mm to $321b with a median market cap of $9.41b.

The strategy is heavily weighted to the information technology sector, accounting for 33.72% of the total portfolio weight. FPX also heavily invests in industrials at 16%, communications at 10.57%, and financials at 9.73%.

FPX can be considered a highly concentrated portfolio strategy; the top 10 holdings account for 46.62% of the total portfolio weight. In contrast, the bottom 10 holdings account for only 0.70% of the total portfolio weight. Top holdings include GE Vernova (GEV) at 9.25%, AppLovin (APP) at 9%, Palantir (PLTR) at 5.43%, and Constellation Energy Corp. (CEG) at 4.60%. Despite information technology being the largest sector weight, the strategy provides significant exposure to companies outside of the technology sector, providing for an appealing diversification strategy.

FPX has performed exceptionally well in the last year as investors rotate into a risk-on, high-beta approach to risk management. This has led to significant growth in the ETF above the S&P 500.

Despite the strong performance, investors should be aware that FPX exhibits significantly greater risk relative to the broader large-cap index. High-growth, high-beta companies may exhibit significantly greater volatility, particularly during periods of market stress, as seen in early 2025.

Looking at 1-year performance across the top holdings, FPX may have been largely driven by the exceptionally strong performance amongst these constituents, amongst other high-performance constituents like Seagate (STX) and CoreWeave (CRWV).

Despite the strong performance over the last year, I believe FPX may realize continued strength going forward as the Federal Reserve eases interest rates, potentially pushing investors into a more risk-on approach. Though I suspect there may be more growth to come, I believe investors should remain cautious given the challenging macroeconomic landscape. If investing in FPX, I recommend applying some measures of risk management in order to protect one’s holdings from drastic volatility. This may include a trailing stop on one’s holdings, allowing for the fund to grow while salvaging a predetermined amount of returns. The percentage of rollback will be determined by the investor’s risk profile; given the inherent volatility in FPX, I recommend a 20-30% trailing stop to ensure that shares are not sold prematurely.

Risks Related to FPX

FPX is a high-beta, passive investment strategy designed to provide investors with exposure to newly public companies, presenting certain risks that must be considered prior to making a final investment decision. New public companies may be considered riskier investments; analyzing the components of the portfolio, FPX has a relatively balanced investment strategy across newer and durable companies. FPX may exhibit higher volatility relative to the broader market index, making the strategy most appropriate for investors with a higher risk appetite. FPX can be considered as a highly concentrated portfolio strategy with a substantial proportion of the weight being spread across the top 10 holdings.

Final Thoughts

FPX is an appealing investment strategy for investors taking a risk-on approach. FPX invests across multiple sectors that may exhibit significant growth in the coming years, making this strategy appropriate for tactical and long-term investors. Given the risky nature of the strategy, I believe it will best be utilized as a satellite fund as part of a diversified equity strategy; I am recommending FPX with a Buy rating with a target allocation of 2-3%.

data.title: JNK: The Credit Cycle
data.description:
Introduction

The SPDR Bloomberg High Yield Bond ETF (NYSEARCA:JNK) is essentially a simple way to enter the world of high-yield US corporate bonds. When you buy the ETF, you get a broad, diversified basket of dollar-denominated corporate debt issues from companies rated below investment grade. The vehicle's proposition is clear: it seeks to convert the credit risk premium into regular income for fund participants, with low costs (0.40%).

Behind the fund, there is an index designed to be investable, which is none other than the Bloomberg U.S. High Yield Very Liquid Index. The name itself gives us a clue to one of the main characteristics of this index: it prioritises issues that are sufficiently large and well traded, filters out convertibles and other less standard formats, and weights by market value, so that issuers with more outstanding debt carry more weight. The index is reviewed and rebalanced regularly, usually every month, to incorporate new placements, redemptions and rating changes. This ensures that the ‘profile’ of the universe remains representative of what can actually be bought and sold.

The ETF replicates this behaviour of the index using representative sampling. It does not need to have every exact line of the index; the important thing is to replicate the risk profile. The management uses four levers: distribution by quality (BB, B, CCC), sector exposure, duration and price characteristics such as coupon, convexity or whether the bond is redeemable before maturity. With this mix, the fund's portfolio should react similarly to the index when Treasury rates move, when credit spreads narrow or widen, or when there are changes in the curve. In addition, the creation and redemption mechanism with authorised participants helps to ensure that the price of the ETF does not deviate from its net asset value and provides additional liquidity on a day-to-day basis.

Profitability generation is best understood if you separate it into two drivers. The first is coupons: as lower-quality issuers, they pay higher interest rates to offset the risk of default, and it is this flow that sustains the fund's monthly distributions (already net of expenses). The second is the price of the bonds, which is driven mainly by the risk premium that the market demands for this segment compared to Treasuries.

When that premium, the famous spread, or OAS, narrows because credit sentiment improves, required yields fall and prices rise; when the spread widens, the opposite occurs. At the same time, risk-free rates also matter: with constant spreads, a fall in the Treasury curve supports prices, and a rise puts pressure on them. In high yield, effective duration is usually moderate, so the pure impact of rates is less than in investment grade, but it does not disappear.

Analysis - Macro Fundamental

In this section, we will analyse some macroeconomic indicators in detail to try to offer the reader an accurate perspective and a sound recommendation on the vehicle in question.

The OAS & SLOOS pair directly describes the credit cycle that most influences the ETF analysed here. The OAS is the spread that the market demands on high-yield bonds relative to Treasuries. When that spread widens, the prices of lower-quality bonds fall and the ETF suffers; when it narrows, prices rise and the ETF reaps that appreciation, in addition to the coupon.

The SLOOS measures whether banks are tightening or loosening lending conditions for businesses and, based on historical analysis, tends to move before the OAS. When both are placed on the same scale, a pattern emerges. Periods of tightening by banks are usually followed, with a lag, by a higher OAS, and periods of normalisation in bank lending are usually accompanied by a lower OAS. This is the basic reading for understanding why JNK alternates between periods of price pressure and periods when carry dominates.

At present, the OAS is trading in the lower-middle range of recent years, with occasional spikes but no sustained stress, while the SLOOS has fallen from its peak tightening levels and is now in more restrictive territory than in the previous decade. In practical terms, this leaves us with a scenario in which the coupon is the main driver of the fund's return and the price will depend on whether the SLOOS continues to ease or, at least, does not rise again. If bank credit tightens again, we would normally see a wider OAS months later and a more volatile JNK.

The combination of OAS and 10-year real yield (TIPS) adds the macro backdrop to this analysis. The real rate acts as the market discount rate: with high real rates, financing is more expensive and risk appetite is lower; with falling real rates, the financial environment is more benign and spreads are more likely to narrow.

In high yield, real rates are not the main driver (that role is played by the OAS), but they do set the direction for the segment as a whole. Furthermore, although the duration of the ETF is usually moderate, the level of the real 10-year tranche influences corporate refinancing and the market's tolerance for fragile balance sheets, which ultimately filters through to spreads.

As for the current situation, the starting point is clear: the real yield on ten-year Treasuries remains high compared to the 2010s, and the spread between high yield and Treasuries is in the low-to-mid range observed since the pandemic. In this environment, the driver behind JNK's total return today is carry (coupons). Additional price gains will only come if the spread continues to narrow. In other words, for the price to add more than just periodic income, the market must demand less premium from lower-quality issuers.

The real rate acts as a boundary condition. When the real 10-year rate falls, financing becomes cheaper, financial conditions improve and spreads tend to narrow, allowing the ETF to advance in two ways at once (income and capital appreciation). When the real rate rebounds and, in addition, banks tighten lending standards again according to the SLOOS survey, the market tends to demand a higher premium for high yield, the spread widens and the price of the ETF moves against the tide. Hence the usefulness of reading both pieces together: use the SLOOS trajectory to anticipate where the spread may go and use the real 10-year rate as a filter to indicate whether the macro context favours or hinders that compression.

Conclusion

With the high yield spread against Treasuries moving in the low-mid range of recent levels, bank credit less tight than in past peaks (though still more restrictive than in the previous decade) and the real ten-year rate high, the recommendation is to hold a moderate position in JNK if the main objective is monthly income (carry), avoiding aggressive purchases at these levels. Thank you for reading.

data.title: LVMH: Luxury's Luster Dims, Stock Still Too Expensive, Sell
data.description:
Intro

Louis Vuitton Moët Hennessy, or in brief just LVMH (OTCPK:LVMHF) (OTCPK:LVMUY) was founded in 1987 through the merger of Moët Hennessy and Louis Vuitton. Today, it is the world's luxury goods powerhouse. Listed on Euronext Paris under the ticker MC.PA, the company has grown into the biggest luxury company under Bernard Arnault’s leadership.

LVMH manages over 80 prestigious brands and operates across five core segments: Wines & Spirits (e.g., Moët & Chandon, Hennessy, Dom Pérignon), Fashion & Leather Goods (Louis Vuitton, Christian Dior, Fendi, Loro Piana), Perfumes & Cosmetics (Parfums Christian Dior, Guerlain), Watches & Jewelry (TAG Heuer, Tiffany, Bulgari), and Selective Retailing (Sephora, DFS). Its business model focuses on crafting aspirational products, targeting affluent consumers worldwide. LVMH’s strength lies in its broad brand portfolio, and global retail network, which drive its reputation for exclusivity.

Despite its historical success, LVMH, like almost the entire luxury sector, is coping with challenges from macroeconomic shifts and weakened consumer sentiment, impacting its business and stock performance. That's why shares fell about 40% from their all-time high in 2023.

With a robust financial position and a focus on long-term growth, LVMH remains a luxury behemoth navigating a complex global landscape. At least that's the plan.

I remain cautious, though, given the company still has margins above historical norms, earnings and cash flows diverge, and even using this elevated level, the multiples do not offer a margin of safety. Not to mention that the current negative business trend has not seen any stabilization or even a turnaround, yet.

LVMH stock is a sell until a more favorable risk / reward setup materializes.

Main

LVMH’s first-half 2025 performance reveals a mixed landscape shaped by macroeconomic headwinds and regional disparities. The company posted a revenue decline of 4% for the first half. This was primarily driven by weakened demand in Asia, particularly China, and a sharp reversal in Japan’s tourism-driven sales. Profit from recurring operations dropped by 15%.

Especially LVMH's biggest (by sales) and highest-margin segment, Fashion & Leather Goods, performed poorly. This segment generates 50% of the company's sales and more than 70% of its profit from recurring operations. Unfortunately, it is this segment that has strong momentum to the downside.

Not only are the days of high-growth over, at least for the time being, recent negative growth even accelerated. On the positive side, LVMH maintained high profitability through disciplined cost management and a focus on premium products. Profit margins from recurring operations came were strong at 34%.

Some more positives, Louis Vuitton’s new cosmetic line and partnerships, like with Real Madrid, reinforced its brand allure. Christian Dior’s creative overhaul under Jonathan Anderson sparked excitement, with new bag lines gaining traction. Sephora, within Selective Retailing, outperformed expectations, capturing market share in the U.S., Europe, and the Middle East through exclusive brands and engaging store experiences.

Wines & Spirits (6.5% of sales, 6% of profits) faced pressure from tariff uncertainties impacting cognac demand in the U.S. and China, though Champagne brands like Veuve Clicquot saw improved trends in Europe.

Perfumes & Cosmetics (10% of sales, 5% of profits)) remained stable, with Christian Dior excelling in selective markets like China, driven by new fragrances and makeup innovations.

Watches & Jewelry (13% of sales, 8% of profits) held steady, with Tiffany and Bulgari benefiting from iconic collections and store renovations, though watches lagged due to softer demand.

LVMH’s strategic focus on product innovation, selective retail investments, and cost efficiencies mitigated some pressures. But the trend is clearly to the downside, as all these efforts cannot offset the drag. This approach to keep investments up preserved historically high margins, particularly in Fashion & Leather Goods, and supported strong cash flow generation. The latter was supported by positive working capital effects.

The following chart shows that operating margins have started to come down. First, on a yearly basis (ending with FY 2024).

And a bit more pronounced on a quarterly basis (rolling twelve months).

In both cases, we see operating margins are still above the last trough. This does not mean they must revisit that level, but it is a risk to have in the back of the head. The reason is, the luxury sector is heavily dependent and reliant on physical stores. This produces high fixed costs. Should sales be under pressure for longer than expected, at some point in time, the company won't be able to keep margins on this high level.

Likewise from the call, LVMH shifted focus to local clientele, reducing reliance on volatile tourist spending (caused issues in China and Japan), a move that proved effective in markets like China. Tariff risks, particularly in Wines & Spirits, loom large, with limited flexibility to adjust pricing or supply chains in this segment. Fashion & Leather Goods, however, can leverage U.S. production and moderate price adjustments to navigate potential tariffs.

Looking ahead, LVMH remains cautiously optimistic. Easier comparisons in China and ongoing brand initiatives provide hope, but macroeconomic uncertainties and tough U.S. comparables from last year’s post-election surge cloud the outlook somewhat. A mixed bag so to speak. The Chinese market’s recovery remains sluggish. Still, LVMH’s leadership is confident in its brands’ desirability and competitive edge, expecting a rebound once headwinds ease.

The question is only, how much negativity is priced into the stock?

Valuation – Still elevated

Free cash flow so far has been strong.

But this is mainly the result of modestly reduced Capex and less negative working capital effects. Important to know, the higher FCF was not from internal or organic strength. Operating income has been showing a divergence for several quarters that cannot go on forever this way.

If and when the sector continues to experience sluggish business results, and what I am expecting, margins start to crack more noticeably, this will pressure operating and free cash flow.

But even using the current number of €15 billion, with an enterprise value of €278 billion (net debt only €8 billion), we are still dealing with an EV / FCF of 19x. Plenty of hot air is already out of this stock, I admit that. But I am calculating here with a rather optimistic scenario that assumes a recovery takes place and the company returns to solid growth, not just some low single-digit figures. Currently, I don't see that happening. With margins at risk and free cash flow likely not being able to remain at €15 billion, the multiple only goes higher, making the stock effectively more expensive.

Everyone must decide whether this is really the Big Bang for the buck. For me, it clearly is not. I see the stock as too expensive, and wouldn't be surprised to see it over the next 12 months falling another 15–20%.

I would likewise not assume that the dividend is safe on this level, but this just as a side note.

Risks to my Thesis

My bearish outlook on LVMH assumes continued macroeconomic pressures and overvaluation, but risks to this lonely-wolf thesis exist (no sell ratings from recent coverage).

A faster-than-expected recovery in China could boost local demand, lifting the key segment Fashion & Leather Goods, but also Perfumes & Cosmetics. Favorable tariff outcomes could stabilize margins, particularly in Wines & Spirits. LVMH’s innovation pipeline, including Louis Vuitton’s beauty venture and Dior’s creative momentum, may drive stronger-than-anticipated sales. Sephora’s market share gains could further offset weaknesses elsewhere.

Finally, LVMH’s still strong cash flow and brand dominance might attract investors, supporting share prices despite high valuations, challenging the expected downside.

Conclusion

LVMH’s luxury empire and its stock over-sparkled over the last years. Despite the 40% drop, the stock price remains a costly couture piece in a shaky market. With shares trading at not attractive enough valuations, the risk-reward balance tilts toward further declines.

I rate LVMH a “sell” until valuations align with reality.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Seizing The Growth Opportunities Of AI Transformation
data.description:
By Matt Freund, CFA & Michael Kassab, CFA

The US equity markets continued to rebound from their April lows and generated strong gains in the third quarter, demonstrating remarkable resilience in the face of persistent uncertainties. While growth sectors led the recovery, gains were broad-based, as concerns about tariffs and economic conditions continued to fade.

The quarter began with the passage of the Administration's landmark tax legislation, which made existing tax rates permanent, added new individual tax credits, and provided incentives for capital investment in the United States. Tariff-related inflation fears proved overstated as supply chains adjusted and absorbed much of the cost increases. While the economy continued to grow at a healthy pace, labor market softening provided the Federal Reserve room to begin reducing short-term interest rates. Consequently, rates declined across the yield curve.

Looking ahead, we believe markets are appropriately pricing in an improving earnings outlook. We expect fiscal support will strengthen through 2026 as consumers benefit from higher tax refunds and reduced withholding rates. At the same time, we view market expectations for additional rate cuts as reasonable and expect short-term rates to move toward 3% over the next 12 to 18 months.

From an investment standpoint, we continue to believe artificial intelligence (AI) represents one of the most significant investment opportunities of this decade, with the potential to drive productivity gains across the entire economy. As the market transitions from pure infrastructure buildout to identifying companies that can monetize AI capabilities, we see compelling opportunities emerging across multiple layers of the AI ecosystem. Beyond the established infrastructure providers, we’re identifying promising investments in AI-enabled software platforms, companies leveraging AI for operational efficiency, and businesses that can demonstrate sustainable competitive advantages through proprietary data and models. We’re particularly focused on companies showing tangible returns on AI investments through revenue acceleration, margin expansion, or market share gains rather than those simply benefiting from the capital expenditure cycle.

The combination of positive GDP growth, declining interest rates, and earnings momentum should create a supportive backdrop for risk assets. However, while our outlook for US equities remains favorable — supported by genuine innovation and economic resilience — we remain mindful of key risks. These include a potential reacceleration of inflation that could pause Fed rate cuts, slower-than-expected consumer spending if labor market softness persists, and the possibility that AI monetization timelines extend longer than current market expectations.

Given the extended rally, we are committed to maintaining a disciplined, risk-managed approach to growth investing, focusing on quality companies with reasonable valuations and visible earnings catalysts. We expect market leadership to broaden as AI benefits flow through to margins across sectors, cyclical stocks gain momentum, and out-of-favor areas (like healthcare) regain investor interest. As always, our commitment to prudent portfolio management means staying vigilant, diversified, and focused on balancing risks and opportunities.

Original Post

data.title: Expand Energy: Solid Haynesville Results And Potentially $3+ Billion In 2026 FCF
data.description:
While Roth brought up some concerns around six weeks ago about natural gas oversupply (and thus believed that 2026 strip prices were too high), overall production appears to have eased up a bit. Natural gas storage levels are still 171 Bcf above the five year average, but this is a 25 Bcf decrease in the amount above the five year average since Roth's report.

I am maintaining my estimate of Expand Energy's (NASDAQ:EXE) value at $120 per share, and believe that it has an excellent position in the Haynesville (an area that is attracting additional attention).

Around 42% of Expand's 2H 2025 production (and 41% of its full year 2025 production) is expected to come from the Haynesville, while it is devoting approximately 54% of its full year D&C capex to that asset. That relative overweighting indicates its importance in Expand's portfolio.

The current strip for 2026 and 2027 NYMEX natural gas is roughly $4 (or slightly above). Despite some weakness in oil prices, Expand may be able to generate $3+ billion (or over $12 per share) in free cash flow per year at current strip for those years.

Haynesville Strength

Expand is growing its Haynesville production during 2025, running a 7 rig drilling program with a bit under $1.2 billion in D&C capex.

Reports indicate that Citadel is in talks to buy some Haynesville assets from Comstock Resources (CRK). If that potential deal goes through, it will be an interesting benchmark for Haynesville asset valuations.

Expand appears to be significantly more capital efficient in the Haynesville than Comstock, although part of that is due to Comstock's exploratory activities in the Western Haynesville. Expand is growing its Haynesville production from late 2024 levels, while Comstock's production has declined a bit.

This is despite relatively similar capex budgets (with Comstock's D&C capex budget around 90% of Expand's 2025 Haynesville D&C capex budget) and Expand's need to maintain a much larger production base (with Haynesville production of 2.925 Bcfe per day in 2025) compared to Comstock's roughly 1.275 Bcfe per day in 2025 production.

Expand has managed to deliver consistently solid production from its Haynesville development program, resulting in that positive capital efficiency.

Potential 2026 Outlook

Expand has talked about averaging 7.5 Bcfe in production during 2026. The current strip for 2026 is now around $60 for WTI oil and $4.05 for NYMEX natural gas.

Expand currently has no hedges for NGLs in 2026 and minimal oil hedges during that period. It does have close to 40% of its natural gas production hedged, although most of those are collars that have an average floor of $3.46. Expand's 2026 hedges currently have around $9 million in positive value.

At current strip, Expand is projected to generate $10.233 billion in 2026 revenues after hedges.

Barrels/Mcf Per Barrel/Mcf $ Million
Natural Gas 2,509,275,000 $3.65 $9,159
NGLs 31,937,500 $24.00 $767
Oil 6,083,333 $49.00 $298
Hedge Value $9
Total $10,233

Thus I now expect Expand to generate $3.053 billion in 2026 free cash flow. Expand's income taxes are expected to be around 70% deferred in 2026, potentially keeping its cash taxes under $200 million.

$ Million
Production Expenses $700
Gathering, Processing, and Transportation $2,800
Production Taxes $300
G&A $225
Net Cash Interest $225
Capital Expenditures $2,750
Cash Taxes $180
Total $7,180

Notes on Valuation

As we can see above, a $4+ NYMEX natural gas environment could allow Expand to generate over $3 billion in free cash flow per year even if oil prices were relatively weak.

A $10 change in oil prices has around the same effect on Expand's free cash flow as a 7.5 cent change in natural gas prices. This includes the potential effect on NGL prices as well, assuming a fixed realized price for Expand's NGLs of 40% of WTI oil.

I am modeling results based on current strip prices until the end of 2026 and then $70 WTI oil and $3.75 NYMEX natural gas after that time. This results in a $120 per share estimated value for Expand.

A scenario using current strip prices until the end of 2027 would be slightly favorable to Expand, as current 2027 strip is roughly $60 to $61 WTI oil and $4.00 NYMEX natural gas. That would add a couple dollars to Expand's estimated value, bringing it up to $122 per share.

Conclusion

Although oil prices have been relatively mediocre recently, the outlook for natural gas prices looks better, with current 2026 and 2027 strip averaging around $4 or slightly higher. Mediocre oil prices may have a modest positive impact on natural gas prices too, due to the effect on associated gas production.

Expand may be able to generate $3+ billion per year in free cash flow in 2026 and 2027 at current strip. Most of its 2026 natural gas hedges are collars with a floor near $3.50 and a ceiling near $5, so it should be able to generate significant free cash flow next year even if prices go down somewhat, while there is upside with improved natural gas prices.

Expand's Haynesville results have been pretty solid and it is devoting a bit over 50% of its D&C capex budget to that asset. Expand's Haynesville production is also slowly increasing as a percentage of its total production.

I am maintaining my estimate of Expand's value at $120 per share (based on current strip until the end of 2026 and $3.75 NYMEX natural gas after that), and would slightly increase that to $122 per share using 2027 strip prices as well.

data.title: Calamos Fixed Income Suite: Positioned For A Steepening Yield Curve
data.description:
By Matt Freund, CFA, Christian Brobst, and Chuck Carmody, CFA

Credit markets performed well in the third quarter, reflected in the Bloomberg US High Yield 2% Issuer Capped Index’s gain of 2.5%. Investors were cheered by a surprisingly resilient economy, fading tariff concerns, and the resumption of the Federal Reserve’s monetary easing program. Despite a growing economy (current GDP estimates are over 3% for the third quarter), job data has been notably softer as companies slow hiring. Additionally, retroactive revisions to the survey showed employment gains were overstated by more than 900,000 jobs (for the year ended March 31, 2025). Nevertheless, the unemployment rate remains low at 4.3%, and jobless claims have moderated after an unusually high print in early September.1 Inflation continues to run around 3%, well above the Fed’s 2% target.

Chair Powell and his Fed colleagues indicated their September 25-basis point rate cut was a "risk management" exercise in the wake of the softer employment backdrop. While additional rate cuts are broadly expected, the question becomes whether the market is overestimating the speed with which they will occur. The yield curve is now pricing a 3% terminal rate for this cycle, to be reached by the first quarter of 2027. That would be five additional cuts merely to get back to neutral (versus the 3% median estimate of the long-term neutral rate in September). Not surprisingly, Fed governors have varying opinions about where the neutral rate lies, with a low-to-high range of 2.625% to 3.875%. A new Fed chair on the horizon introduces a wildcard to the debate.

Both hard economic data and survey results show a bifurcated consumer backdrop. High earners are doing well, continuing to expand consumption and skewing aggregate data. Meanwhile, the bottom half of earners face increased burdens from higher prices for necessities and the impact of policy changes.2 We believe 3% is an appropriate goal for short-term rates and that the timing is less important than the current headlines imply. Lower interest rates, lower tax bills (the President’s landmark tax legislation should result in larger refunds and reduced withholdings in 2026), reduced onerous regulatory burdens, and the resolution of tariff uncertainty should provide a positive economic backdrop. Even so, it is unclear if the distribution of benefits will match the distribution of the costs of higher import prices and reduced healthcare subsidies. That said, so long as layoff activity remains low, we believe that the US economy will avoid a growth recession in 2026.

Corporate fundamentals are moderating as margins continue to decline and leverage metrics are increasing. Even so, balance sheets remain healthy, the fundamental backdrop is still solid, capital market liquidity is abundant, and the overall credit quality of the high yield market is above average. These factors contribute to a default rate below long-term averages. We expect default activity to remain muted into 2026.

The technical backdrop remains very strong, as evidenced by significant inflows into high-yield and loan funds, limited net new debt issuance, and anecdotally strong demand for most new issues. The combination of solid fundamentals and strong technicals has led to credit spreads trading near the tightest levels witnessed in recent years.

Positioning Implications

The Fed faces a challenging task with both sides of its dual mandate out of balance simultaneously, and a patient approach to raising rates driven by fear of a mistake may result in analysis paralysis. Regardless, an active easing cycle and Fed independence, combined with structural challenges around fiscal deficits, term premia, and inflation uncertainty, are likely to keep the curve steepening bias in place.

These expectations result in Calamos Short-Term Bond Fund (CSTIX) having a duration long of its benchmark, while that of Calamos Total Return Bond Fund (CTRIX) is neutral to its benchmark. In Calamos High Income Opportunities Fund (CIHYX), where interest rate sensitivity has a lower impact on returns, the fund is positioned short of benchmark duration.

Tight trading ranges in credit spreads should hold as long as technical factors remain supportive. Nevertheless, spread compression across rating categories has allowed the team to migrate credit quality higher across strategies in recent months while maintaining an attractive level of income. We will continue to position the funds defensively until relative value opportunities to move down in credit improve.

Footnotes

1 While the data is preliminary, it appears changes to immigration policies may be reducing the number of workers looking for employment at the same time employers are curtailing hiring.

2 One notable example is the resumption of credit reporting for federal student loans, which is negatively impacting credit scores and reducing access to credit.

Original Post

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Is The High-Beta Train On The Right Track For Long-Term Investors?
data.description:
High-Beta Stocks Have Surged, but Quality Stocks Have Staying Power

US equity markets have been on a roll in recent months, but what’s been fueling the gains? Market returns have been driven by riskier stocks, while quality has taken a back seat. While the runaway high-beta train might give investors an exciting ride, it might not be the best mode of transport for a longer investment journey.

Since early April, the US stock market’s surge has been driven by high-beta stocks, a relatively risky group. Meanwhile, stocks with higher-quality features have lagged behind. These include stocks that rank high on dividend yield, dividend growth and shareholder yield (i.e., companies that consistently return capital via dividends and share buybacks).

Thrill Rides Don’t Last Forever

The short-term ride may be thrilling, but will it last? Longer-term data tell a different story. Over the last 20 years, higher-quality stocks have been much more resilient than high-beta stocks. The top quintile of stocks with high shareholder yield and high dividend growth have returned 57.3% and 61.7% more than the lowest quintile, respectively, over the last two decades; the highest-beta stocks have returned 10.9% less than their lowest quintile peers.

Current risks warrant caution. Considering the lofty valuations of the S&P 500 and other US indices, as well as risks such as stubborn inflation and geopolitical tensions, we believe higher-volatility stocks may be more vulnerable to a sharp pullback than higher-quality stocks. Expectations of more Fed interest rate cuts could also prompt a rebound in these capital return-oriented stocks, in our view. In a world facing disruption on many fronts, companies with resilient business models and disciplined capital returns courtesy of thoughtful management teams could reassert themselves as quiet outperformers for patient investors with a long time horizon.

The views expressed herein do not constitute research, investment advice or trade recommendations and do not necessarily represent the views of all AB portfolio-management teams. Views are subject to revision over time.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Garmin: A Solid Company But A Little Rich For Me
data.description:
Introduction

Garmin (NYSE:GRMN) is due to report its Q3 numbers at the end of the month, and since I haven’t looked at the company in a while, I thought I’d go through what to expect and what I am looking for in general regarding the company’s operations and key growth drivers.

I will continue to remain on the sidelines for now, as I would like to see what kind of potential the company has based on the management’s comments in the future, and the overall economic outlook.

What To Expect

Analysts are expecting GRMN to do $1.98 in EPS on $1.79B in sales, which translates to around a slight sequential decline and around 13% growth y/y. That’s not a bad growth overall; however, the EPS number is slightly lower compared to last year, by around 10 cents, which means the company’s expenses are slightly higher. How likely is the company to beat these estimates? Over the last 10 quarters, GRMN only missed on EPS estimates once, while beating revenue estimates 100% of the time.

Unfortunately, the company doesn’t provide quarterly guidance numbers, only full numbers, and based on the guidance for 2025, the expectation on EPS for Q3 should be around $2, which is what analysts are saying in general.

Looking at the numbers, it seems that estimates are usually quite a ways away from the actual results, which means it is hard to ever tell what the company will do when it doesn’t provide a quarterly outlook. It seems that analysts are usually on the lower end, probably based on the management’s more subdued commentary regarding the outlook in general. It could be a case of the management lowballing comments/full-year estimates to consistently beat expectations.

I think it is very safe to say that the company will once again beat estimates on the top and bottom lines. Additionally, I wouldn’t be too surprised if the company raises its full-year guidance once again, as it did in the last two quarters this year.

Overall, I expect decent quarter numbers and probably will be on the better side of things than not.

What I’m Looking For

The company’s operating margins have seen a tremendous improvement in the previous quarter. Q2 ’25 showed around 26% vs. 22% last year. A 400bps increase in one year is admirable. Based on analysts’ expectations, we may see some margin contraction in the upcoming quarter, or we’ll see the company beat the expectations out of the water, and the margin improvements will continue. If we do see some contraction, it would be good to know why that is the case. Is it tariff-related? Is it something else? We’ll know soon enough.

The summer quarter showed a fantastic top-line growth of around 20%, which helped its margins. A favorable product mix shift also contributed to such an annual improvement in profitability.

The company’s fitness segment has seen some amazing growth recently, with over 40% y/y just this past quarter. I would like to see if this momentum will be sustained going forward and what products will help the company achieve such growth in the future. The demand was driven by growth in advanced wearable devices like the Forerunner 570 and 970, the Venu X1 smartwatch, and new products for monitoring health. Also, it helped that the past quarter was essentially the midsummer season, so many people were looking to get back into fitness to offset any of the BBQ evenings. I don’t think we’ll see 40% growth performance in this segment in the upcoming quarter, but I still expect a decent number, maybe around 25%. Anything below that may be seen as a negative by the investing community.

The outdoor segment might still have some juice in growth going forward. Last quarter, this segment grew around 11%, with strong demand in adventure watches and rugged GPS handheld navigators, like the eTrex Touch. The weather is still decent, and will include some of July and August, so people were still looking to have some outdoor fun. This segment has really healthy gross margins at around 66% and operating margins at nearly 32%, so I will be looking at how these numbers have progressed since the last quarter and whether we should be worried. I don’t think so right now.

Regarding the marine and aviation segments, I expect the performance that we have seen over the last quarters to remain the same. Garmin has a really strong grip on these two segments and is a leader in these spaces. The acquisition of Lumishore last year should help GRMN maintain its position within the marine segment. However, given the leadership here, I would like to see some margin improvements going forward.

The automotive OEM segment has seen some decent top-line growth recently. Growth led by increased shipments of domain controllers. It is still, however, operating at a loss, and I would like to hear what the progress is like to achieve profitability. We know that we should expect top-line growth to be around 10% for 2025, but I would like to get some idea of when this is going to become profitable.

I will also be looking out for any type of AI buzz. The company is increasingly integrating AI into its wearables and health platforms. The new Venu 4 smartwatch features AI-powered health analytics is one way a company is trying to keep up with the competition, as well as the collaboration with Meta (META), should, in turn, generate some AI hype for companies that were not previously known as AI plays. I wouldn’t call GRMN an AI play just yet due to the near-term impact being rather low; however, I wouldn’t rule it out in the medium-term.

Valuation

Let’s take a simple look at some assumptions regarding the company’s outlook. Below are the assumptions I will be using to keep it simple.

The company expects to finish the year with around 10% growth in the top line, and analysts, on average, have around 7% growth for the upcoming years. I decided to go with around 10% for the next 5 years. I wanted to give some more credit to the company’s ability to achieve double-digit growth in sales. Interestingly, the company’s 5-year CAGR is around 10%, while the long-term has been slightly higher.

I am also assuming the company’s operating margins will increase further to 27% over the next 5 years, while all other metrics seem to be fine, like the working capital change, D&A, and Capex, so I will stick with historical metrics. I will also be using the company’s WACC of 8.3%. I like to use 10% for a bit of an extra margin of safety, but the company isn’t particularly volatile, so I am fine with its WACC as my discount rate.

With that said, the company at this stage is rather expensive for me to start a position. My model says it is a strong sell, but if I were already an investor in GRMN, I wouldn’t be selling it and would continue to accumulate on reasonable dips.

Closing Comments

Garmin has had a decent one-year run overall. The share price has gone up by over 50%, which is most likely thanks to the company’s profitability improvements and decent top-line growth.

Do I think it is a buy right now, right before earnings? No, not for me it isn’t. I think that the company will continue to perform as it has in the past, and I would like to add to my portfolio someday, but I would like to see a decent pullback before I would consider it a buy. I don’t think the upcoming quarter is going to change my view that much; therefore, I am maintaining a hold rating for now.

I would like to see the company hitting much higher operating margins over the next few years before I would consider looking into the company once again, especially if its share price continues to rise while margins stagnate.

data.title: Hapag-Lloyd: Looking At An Entry Below €100/Share
data.description:
Hapag-Lloyd (OTCPK:HPGLY) (OTCPK:HLAGF) is a shipper I've stayed neutral on since my coverage started. My preference for the segment tends towards the bigger alternative, Maersk (OTCPK:AMKBY), where I actually have an investment. But that just means that what decides its appeal is the company's valuation. If Hapag-Lloyd goes to a cheap enough price, I would absolutely be interested in entering this investment. And since I raised my PT in my last article, which you can find here, and the company reported 2Q25 recently, about a month and a half ago, I believe it's a good time to provide an update here.

If you follow my work, you know I run a very well-diversified portfolio. Very few positions are above 1-2%. This is both related to the amount of capital involved in the portfolio and because I like diversification for reduced risk. I'm "okay" with a lower overall portfolio return in exchange for safety. My overall target is beating my comparative index (if you don't beat the index, you might as well buy an index fund), but I'm not really interested in prospects marketed as "1000%+ growth in 2 years" due to the implied risks of such moves.

Hapag-Lloyd might be volatile and correlated to the shipping industry - much like Maersk - but it's not "massively risky" as such. It's one of the largest shipping companies on the planet - and the 2Q25 period does come with some positives.

You can find my last article on the company here.

Hapag-Lloyd Upside After Shipping Price Declines In 2Q25

It's clear that the company is experiencing the same slowdown in freight rates as its competition. Sales grew 11% for the period due to volumes, but freight rates in themselves were down, leading to a lower guidance from management for the full-year period. Also, a warning that yes, the slowdown could in fact worsen, which would obviously see even lower potential rates - and this is, what I believe caused the earnings-day decline of almost 8% for the company.

I was ready at the time, but decided, after reviewing targets and assumptions, not to invest at that specific point in time - but to keep my options open.

It's worth mentioning, though, because I haven't reviewed Maersk yet, that Hapag-Lloyd's peer instead increased its guidance, not due to freight rates, but due to significant potential volume growth, which caused some eyebrow raising on my part. Maersk also spoke about resiliency on the demand side - again, worth considering here.

The current estimate is a volume growth in container shipping of 3% from Hapag-Lloyd, while Maersk now expects 2-4%. This makes sense. The difference is how they expect earnings to evolve from this.

None of these demand trends change the overall picture of the shipping industry, which I stand by is as follows. The shipping industry has been underinvested since about 2010 - meaning there's a limited supply of vessels. We're now in a situation where the order for vessels hasn't been at this high level since 2011, but where it will still take time for that new supply to hit the market. Once it does, though, it will result in the shipping market, globally, seeing an oversupply. This should reduce freight rates. The fact that global freight rates can be considered a commodity that Hapag-Lloyd does not control means that we should view the company's appeal based on a fairly strict perspective in terms of what the broader market offers for freight, and premiumization doesn't really make sense.

Where Hapag-Lloyd can "earn extra" is in its services and ancillary port and other services. And here it has been investing heavily. Terminals have been an interesting addition - because Hapag-Lloyd now owns 20, and plans to own 35 by 2030. This will offer increased resilience in earnings and better visibility. This is not only a key point for the hub-and-spoke model the company has entered into with Maersk, but I view these additions as more positive than the general market (where there's some doubt as to their ability to materially affect company earnings over time). The investments in terminals will, in my view, improve inland services, customer relationships, and enable the company to capture a better portion of customer spending. I believe we've only seen the beginning of this.

Specific challenges for freight in 1H25 include the US tariff policy development. Volume saw increases, but this increase was mainly driven by increased volume from EMEA, not from NA.

As you can see above, rates surged due to the tariff pause, only to then decline again, as demand from the US has slowly been normalizing. Due to the Red Sea issues, vessels are also still being rerouted over South Africa and the Cape of Good Hope. Most shippers continue to view a return to the Red Sea as unlikely, given the continued attacks on vessels by the Houthi militia.

Tariffs continue to be one of the primary issues and impacts for shippers as well. There's a high uncertainty here, which is lowering typical import season trends. While implementation has caused some stability, there's an ongoing threat of further tariffs dampening sentiment. US imports continue to account for around 15% of global container traffic, and it will be interesting to see just how this develops going forward.

Internally, the company has successfully moved into the Gemini model.

The overall picture I have for Hapag-Lloyd is that the company actually did better in 1H25 than the market reaction to its share price would suggest that it did. I believe a double-digit volume, revenue is good, despite a decline in EBITDA, and I believe the resilient FCF is something to consider with the company as well.

Earnings weren't just lowered by unfavorable freight rates, either, but by things like operational costs in ports, and still costs for network transitions and the like. And after everything, we do need to consider that the company's main segments still generate upwards of $700-$900M in EBITDA on a quarterly basis, or over $3B per year. These results are not bad, especially when considering how heavy the impacts of ship rerouting, tariffs, and incoming capacity issues are. The company's hub-and-spoke unit cost advantages are currently being "hidden" by those aforementioned rerouting costs. Once these reroutes are done, we should be able to see far better trends for the company overall. To give you an idea, the company expects a full billion USD of savings by 2026, which should improve this materially going forward.

The challenges saw a slight increase in company leverage, but the company's balance sheet remains incredibly strong with a liquidity reserve of over $7B. To say that this company is at any financial risk would be going much too far (given that the net debt was actually negative at the year-end of 2024).

Bears would argue that Hapag-Lloyd's success in the hub-and-spoke model is replicable by other companies - I would argue that this takes time and money, as evidenced by the company's own costs - and that the players with the necessary size to actually deliver this are few and far between. And Hapag-Lloyd and Maersk are already working on theirs.

This remains one of the largest shippers, with a spot in the second-largest alliance on the ocean.

Valuation For Hapag-Lloyd - Plenty To Like At The Right Price

Unfortunately, while I like the company, I can't like the valuation for the company. With tariffs uncertain and the overcapacity coming to the market, I'm unwilling to go above a forward EV/EBITDA multiple of 6x, which is typically what Hapag-Lloyd trades for or what it has traded for. Despite my positivity on the terminals segment, the fact is that liner services will remain the company's core business - and while volumes for the company are slightly above market growth rates, this does not justify an entry at the current native share price, which is over €115/share, or almost at a 7.5x EV/EBITDA.

In the end, how you value Hapag-Lloyd or Maersk depends on your outlook on freight prices. In my DCF model for the company, I assume an average freight rate of $1,325 in 2025, above the longer-term assumption of $1,220 because of the Red Sea disruption. I also believe the freight prices to go down over time, given later capacity additions. Even with a lower revenue growth due to capacity additions, which is the current forecast, the company will see improvements in its net income margins based on the hub-and-spoke model, with as high as 3.9-4.0% possible, which will boost the potential fair value and normalized EPS to, at the very least, mid-€4/share and potentially over €5/share.

The second impact on my valuation model is tariffs as a potential; a prolonged tariff war between the NA, EU, and China could drop volumes by 2-3% overall, and this would drive my estimate down below €90/share for the company during this time. It could be even worse if we move into a global recession, but I consider the risk of this to be remote. I use two different scenarios for tariffs: either a "normalized" short recession from a tariff war lasting sub-12 months (numbers above), or a longer-term 18+ month tariff war if some of the current political ambitions against China are enacted.

Some of the reasoning as to why I don't discount the company further is reliability. Hapag-Lloyd has a far higher reliability than its competitors - above 90%, with peers between 50% and 70%.

This is the reason why I continue to apply a 6x-6.5x EV/EBITDA range for the company.

With the company's dividend, I consider this as follows. Hapag-Lloyd currently has a native dividend yield of just south of 7%. While the dividend itself is paid out is tendentially well-covered on the basis of a very solid leverage (almost no debt), and the company never really "overpays" what it can't afford, the amount of the dividend is variable, as it is with its peer Maersk. Because of this, I give the company a dividend rating that's "safe" - but you have to be aware of the variable amount. 2024A numbers, the latest fiscals we have, put the yield at that sub-7% level, but I consider it likely that the dividend will be lower for the next fiscal, given where things seem to be going in earnings. The company's shareholder distribution policy is what I would consider to be "fair" - 30% of group profit, which allows for high payouts during high periods, but lower nominal payouts during lower periods. Dividends are also not impacted by share buybacks, because Hapag-Lloyd typically does not do them. The growth has been variable, but over a 5-year period, the company's payout has increased by about 50% in terms of the portion paid out.

The company, like Maersk, is very volatile. We can see this in the past 5 years of freight prices, which have JoJo'ed significantly. It's not just tariffs, but completely unforeseeable events like the Red Sea situation, necessitating significant re-planning and increased costs. It's not just political or militia risks as well, but risks to logistics can come from something like the environment. Take 2024, where, due to significant climate impacts in Panama, the number of ships through the Panama Canal was limited.

Other analysts tend to agree with me in terms of targets. Hapag-Lloyd is between a €72/share low-end target and a €132/share high-end, with an average of €107 - but out of 11 analysts following the company, it's somewhat reticent in terms of recommendations, to put it mildly.

Alternatives are really only one - Maersk - and while this company hasn't really moved "down" much, it's still at what I consider to be a more appealing entry than Hapag-Lloyd, even if not by much. If pressed, I would consider Maersk the better entry here with at least a double-digit potential upside - but at the same time, I would caution investors in the sector to look at other sectors and areas for investment instead. Because the fact is, there are more attractive ones out there at this time - such as industrials, some utilities, and the like.

For that reason, my risk considerations are as follows.

Risks & Considerations

The risk you take for the company by following my "Hold" target is company outperformance. It's entirely possible that Hapag-Lloyd would move in a higher direction - it's already trading at over €115/share at the time of writing this article. If the market were to value Gemini higher, and the investments in the hubs and terminals were to turn out better, then this company could go higher.

However, I consider these risks remote next to the downside risk you'd take by investing in this company at this price, well over my 6-6.1x EV/EBITDA target in an environment where top-line results are likely to move down in the near term.

My thesis update for 2Q25 is therefore as follows, and my €98/share PT for HPLGY comes to $58/share.

Thesis

The risks to the company on a fundamental level are very low due to strong fundamentals and a superb balance sheet without much debt. Even in the case of macro issues, Hapag-Lloyd is going nowhere.

That being said, there's a general lack of visibility in this entire sector, which has always made shipping a tricky investment. For this reason, and for the reason of investment alternatives, I consider Hapag-Lloyd to be a "HOLD" at a higher valuation, and the higher valuation is exactly what we have here.

I therefore reiterate for the company my conservative PT of €98/share for the German native ticker. Given the share price, this makes it a "HOLD." I would wait for a better entry point.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is qualitative
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside based on earnings growth or multiple expansions/reversions.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators, and 0 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 3/5 "stars" here, which typically isn't enough. Based on this, and a valuation target of €98/share, I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me. I give the company a "Hold" rating as of October 2025.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Argan Remains An Attractive Growth Play
data.description:
In early June of this year, I made a decision that many in this world find to be difficult. And that was to admit that I was wrong. Specifically, I viewed myself as being wrong about Argan (NYSE:AGX), an interesting business that plays in the construction space and that caters to the power industry, as well as to certain aspects of the industrial economy. In the past, I had been bullish about the company. But after seeing the stock rise 289.3% compared to the 35.2% that the S&P 500 sold during the time that I had it rated a 'buy,' I subsequently downgraded it to a 'hold' only to see the stock rise another 46.9% while the S&P 500 dipped 1.9%.

Based on the rapid growth that the company had experienced, I came to believe the downgrading was premature. And as a result, in my most recent article in June, I upgraded it back to a 'buy' candidate. Since then, the business has continued to achieve interesting and generally positive results. Backlog is particularly promising. The stock has performed well, rising 14.3% at a time when the S&P 500 is up 12.2%. To be clear, shares are anything but cheap. And if we project out based on what we have seen already for this year, this stock is trading at levels that I would typically associate with being in the fair value range. But considering the growth prospects that the business offers, I think that maintaining it as a 'buy' candidate is the right move.

Still Bullish

Since my previous article about Argan was published, investors have only had data covering one additional operating quarter come out. This would be the second quarter of the 2026 fiscal year. During that time, revenue for the company totaled $237.7 million. This represents an increase of 4.7% compared to the $227 million that the company reported the previous year. This rise in revenue was driven almost entirely by the firm's Power Industry Services segment, which focuses on things like engineering, procurement, construction, project commissioning, maintenance, technical consulting, and more, involving the power generation industry. It caters to public utilities, independent power producers, power plant equipment suppliers, and more.

During the most recent quarter, revenue for this segment came in at $196.9 million. That's an increase of 13.3% over the $173.8 million the company reported the previous year. According to management, this was thanks to an uptick in construction activities thanks to the 405 MW Midwest Solar Project and the 700 MW Combined-Cycle Project that it is working on. Things would have been even better had it not been for decreased construction activities on several other projects that have either partially or fully come to completion.

By comparison, the other segments for the company really weren't that impressive. The smallest of them, the Telecommunications Infrastructure Services segment, reported a rise in revenue, but only from $3.6 million to $4.7 million. In fact, management didn't really even comment on what caused that increase because of how immaterial that part of the business currently is. More significant, however, is the Industrial Construction Services segment, which focuses on providing field services associated with new plant construction and additions, as well as other similar activities, for industrial plants that are primarily located in the southeast portion of the US. Overall revenue for this segment actually dropped from $49.6 million to $36.1 million. According to management, this is because of field services construction activities and vessel fabrication work declining.

With revenue for the company increasing, it's not shocking to see profitability improve also. Net income, for instance, expanded from $18.2 million to $25.3 million. But it wasn't alone. While operating cash flow did plummet from $73.5 million to $34 million, the picture is different if we strip out changes in working capital. Doing so would give us a near doubling from $20.9 million to $38.7 million. Finally, EBITDA for the business popped from $24.8 million to $36.2 million.

In the chart above, you can see financial results for the first half of the 2026 fiscal year compared to the same time of 2025. As was the case with the second quarter on its own, Argan benefited from rising revenue that resulted in higher profits and cash flows. As the chart below illustrates, two of the three operating segments for the company improved, with the Power Industry Services segment doing the heavy lifting as sales skyrocketed from $284 million to $357.3 million. And once again, the Industrial Construction Services segment would be the disappointment.

While this mixed picture between the two largest of the company's three operating segments may seem disconcerting, the fact of the matter is that, on the whole, Argan is showing impressive signs of growth. Even though the rise in revenue for the most recent quarter was not all that strong on a year-over-year basis, backlog for the company is exploding higher. In the most recent quarter, backlog amounted to $1.95 billion. That's up from the $1.86 billion that the company had in the first quarter of this year. But that's nothing. The backlog reported by management during the most recent quarter is an astounding 88.7% above the $1.04 billion the company had in the second quarter of 2025. It was also 137% greater than what the company saw in the second quarter of 2024.

In my last article about the business, I detailed a significant amount of data regarding the growth levers that the company has. The opportunities include AI and data centers, the growth of which should fuel AI power demand by 70% annually through the year 2027. Electric vehicles and charging infrastructure associated with them should jump significantly as EV adoption grows by 20-fold by the year 2040. And on top of this, major investments in solar technology and battery factories should prove bullish. It's not just growth in demand that will be a driver for the company. There's also the fact that aging infrastructure is being retired and replaced by new infrastructure as well. There was a 65% jump in plant retirements in 2025 compared to 2024. And over the next decade, 78GW of planned retirements are expected to occur.

Another really great thing about the company is that while shares are not exactly cheap, they aren't what I would consider to be expensive either, especially relative to the growth potential that we have here. If we assume that the rest of this year will look like the first half of the year did on a year over year basis, we would get net profits of $189.3 million, adjusted operating cash flow of $184 million, and EBITDA of $205.7 million. With these estimates, we can see in the chart above how shares of the business are currently valued. Especially on a forward basis that incorporates these 2026 estimates, the stock doesn't look bad. In fact, it would normally be in the fair value range in my book. But because of the rapid growth that we are seeing and the strong catalyst for additional growth moving forward, I am more flexible.

In the table above, I then compared the company to five other similar enterprises. Even if we value it using the 2025 figures, the firm is only a bit toward the more expensive side of the spectrum, while using the 2026 estimates, it is mostly near the lower end of the spectrum. This relative valuation is certainly encouraging. On top of this, we also need to take into consideration the fact that the business has a fortress balance sheet. The firm ended the most recent quarter with no debt and enjoyed a cash position of $572.2 million. For a business with a market capitalization as of this writing of $3.12 billion, I would consider this to be significant.

Takeaway

Right now, I firmly believe that Argan is a solid prospect for investors. It might not be the cheapest candidate out there, but it is a quality company that is growing nicely and that should continue to grow for the foreseeable future. Because of this, I believe that maintaining it as a 'buy' candidate is the right choice.
